text: Ladies and gentlemen, in this instruction video for trainees, I will discuss several aspects of the treatment of perianal sepsis. In many hospitals, operative treatment of perianal abscesses is the first procedure trainees are allowed to do on their own. This procedure is unfairly perceived as a simple, foolproof operation. In nineteen sixty one Sir Alan Parkes provided histopathological evidence for the cryptoglandular theory. This theory is convenient and utilized by colorectal surgeons all over the world. Parkes showed the presence of diseased anal glands in ninety percent of his patients. This hypothesis states that anal glands situated in the space between the internal and the external sphincter are obstructed and become inflamed. This inflammation can then cause an intersphincteric abscess. This inflammation can then spread downwards, outwards or even upwards, giving rise to perianal, ischorectal or intersphincteric abscesses. Even though this theory is convenient and likely, please be aware that it has been debated and that evidence disproving it also exists.

There is a wide variation in the way patients are prepared to undergo this procedure. Despite international variation, most European countries, local, regional or general anesthesia is utilized for these patients. All drainages of perianal abscesses can be performed in supine position. In our hospital no skin disinfection is performed and only casual draping is used for abscess draining. The darker pigmentation surrounding the anus delineates the outer border of the external anal sphincter. Usually the location of the abscess is easily recognizable. In this case some spontaneous discharge has already occurred Incision can be made using either knife or cautery. Remember the direction of circular fibers of the external anal sphincter as well as its proximity to the skin. Be aware that an incision perpendicular to these fibers may actually cause damage to it.

The surgeon performs a circumferential incision to drain a superficial perianal abscess, creating an oval extension to ensure proper drainage. The abscess cavity is gently explored for any undrained fluid collections, avoiding vigorous manipulation to prevent bleeding. In cases of ischorectal abscesses, small hemostats may be used to open the abscess, and large bore needle aspiration is considered if necessary.

Remember that active hemostasis is seldomly necessary. General rule of thumb is that more pus than blood should be evacuated. Many surgeons will pack the wound after incising and abscess. There is a huge variation in this practice. Two studies investigating this matter showed no discernible benefits for patients who underwent packing, but they did find more pain. For this reason, Dutch guideline advises against the packing of wounds.

Another dilemma is made up by the question whether or not a fistulotomy should be performed at the time of abscess drainage. If an internal opening can be found fistulotomy is fairly simple. There is little evidence available whether or not fistulotomy at this time is beneficial or if it may even be harmful. We do know that after fistulotomy less than four percent of patients will develop a fistula, whereas twenty-five to forty percent will develop a fistula if no primary fistulotomy is performed. Even though a recent Cochrane review did not show significant impairment of continence, incontinence has been described after primary fistulotomy for perianal abscess. Evidence justifies selective application of this technique in confirmed low fistulas. However, the prudent trainee will live by these commandments.

Never perform fistulotomy without consent. Never perform fistulotomy in patients with diminished continence and always discuss with your attending surgeon or supervisor. If you decide to look for an internal opening, try to identify the internal opening by intra-anal inspection. The probing from the external side in heavily suppurated tissue may give rise to false identification of the internal opening. Especially in the case of interesting cleric abscesses, the internal opening can be easily identified. In selected cases, enlarging this opening can be adequate treatment. However, these patients need close follow-up to rule out persisting abscesses.
Thank you very much for your attention. I hope you found this video useful.
